quotes.text: The ancient prophet Micah perhaps surprisingly seemed to rule out the nearest and most normal sources of assistance—family, friends, and leaders. Some of us have perhaps experienced a measure of the deep disappointment he felt because of Israel’s rebelliousness when he declared that “the good man is perished out of the earth” (Micah 7:2). He spoke of princes and judges asking for rewards, and of great men uttering “mischievous desire” (v. 3). For Micah, the source of help was clear and sure: “Therefore I will look unto the Lord,” he said. “I will wait for the God of my salvation: my God will hear me” (v. 7).
quotes.speaker: Marion D. Hanks
quotes.attr: Marion D. Hanks, in Conference Report, Oct. 1986, 12; or Ensign, Nov. 1986, 11

notes: In Mic 7:5, who is the woman referred to here? A: This woman should be the listener’s wife. The language is unusual here, implying a distance, separation.

quotes.text: We see much that is glorious and reassuring in good human beings, but mortal men have limitations. None of us has ever met a mortal in whom we could comfortably rest our salvation. Only one qualifies for that trust, and he is the Holy One of Israel. His love for us was and is so great that he volunteered for the unspeakable burden of carrying the weight of our sins. He is our Mediator and our Advocate with the Father. The prophet Micah spoke truthfully and faithfully long ago when, in a time of great trouble, he testified: “I will look unto the Lord; I will wait for the God of my salvation: my God will hear me” (Micah 7:7).
quotes.speaker: Marion D. Hanks
quotes.attr: Marion D. Hanks, in Conference Report, Oct. 1986, 14; or Ensign, Nov. 1986, 13

notes: He waits to be gracious! He loves to be merciful! The prophets call him “the Father of mercies.” (2 Cor. 1:3 [2 Corinthians 1:3].) They speak of his “abundant mercy,” (1 Pet. 1:3 [1 Peter 1:3]), and declare that “whosoever repenteth, and hardeneth not his heart, he shall have claim on mercy” (Alma 12:34). They declare his “wisdom … mercy, and grace.” (2 Ne. 9:8 [2 Nephi 9:8].) And crowning all of this is the

Nahum was one of the greatest poets of all the prophets. The King James Version does not show this as clearly as does the Hebrew text. His little book of only forty-seven verses is the announcement and proclamation of the fall of Ninevah, the Assyrian Capital. Nahum predicts the destruction, event by event as if he were sitting on the walls of the city at the very moment of the siege. There is an element of rejoicing over such a terrible destruction. This has made some critics evaluate Nahum as an inferior prophet. But when one realizes the decades that Assyrian cruelty had remained unchecked, the righteous indignation felt by Nahum is more readily appreciated. Nahum has been called the "Prophet of Ninevah's Doom," That was his message. Of Nahum himself we only know what can be obtained from the short book he wrote. He was a poet of great brilliance and dramatic power, even comparable to the prophet Isaiah. He was born in the village of Elkosh, but there is no certainty as to where this was located. It may have been a place in the vicinity of ancient Ninevah, and if so, Nahum may have been a descendant of those Israelites taken in the Assyrian captivity of Israel. There are other possibilities for the location of Elkosh, and no positive evidence for any of them has been found (The Voice..., p. 352). The word Nahum means "comforter" or "consoler" but his message in the book of Nahum is anything else but comforting, at least to the Ninevites. Nahum is the "Prophet of Moral Indignation" or the"Prophet of Ninevah's Coming Doom." By the vision and insight given him by the Lord, Nahum announced and proclaimed the horrible fall and destruction of the capitol of that great nation--Assyria. Because his central message is of God's avenging wrath upon that cruel nation, many scholars have said that Nahum is more poetry than prophecy, and have considered it to be an inferior book. It must be emphasized that part of a prophet's work is to rebuke, condemn, denounce sin and pronounce judgment and punishment upon the sinners unless they repent. Nahum knew that God controls the destiny of nations and that by the punishment of the brutal tyrant, so full of cruelty, rapacity, deceitful diplomacy, and commercial trickery, his (God's) morality and justice are manifested. Nahum could therefore rejoice over Ninevah's fall. Nahum describes in vivid language (2:1-10) the siege, capture, and plunder of Ninevah. He describes the

NAHUM This book is a vivid prediction of the approaching downfall of Nineveh, the capital city of Assyria, one of the most warlike of the ancient heathen nations. Of the Prophet Nahum, whose name means "consolation" or "comfort", little is known. His purpose was to comfort his people, long harassed by Assyria, with the promise that this cruel and oppressing people would soon meet destruction at God’s hand.

quotes.text: Nahum gave his book the title "the burden of Nineveh," and its source of information, "the vision of Nahum the Elkoshite." Burden designates it as a message "lifted up" against Nineveh. Vision is used here to translate chazon, which denotes divine vision, not ordinary sight. Nahum means "full of comfort" and could relate to the comfort he brought to Judah in the knowledge of the end of the threat of Assyria. The meaning or location of Elkosh is not known, but tradition holds that ancient Capernaum was the home of this prophet; its Hebrew name, Kephar-Nahum, means "village of Nahum." Nineveh was the capital city of Assyria, which had been a threat to Israel since at least 850 B.C. Assyria finally conquered and exiled northern Israel in 722 B.C. and threatened Judah until Sennacherib's forces were divinely decimated at the end of that century (2 Kgs. 19:20-37). Nahum may have lived about 642 B.C. (BD, "Chronology," p. 639). Fulfillment of Nahum's prophecy came a short time after he gave it, about 614 to 606 B.C., when the mother city of Assyria, Asshur, was destroyed; then Nineveh, the capital city, fell; and finally the rest of the empire capitulated to Nabopolassar, king of the rising empire of Babylon (BD, "Chronology," p. 639). Nahum was another poetic prophet; his language is picturesque and full of symbolism. In his first chapter, he wrote a partially acrostic poem to describe both the destructive might and the abundant goodness of the Lord. At the end of it, he foretold the end of wickedness and heralded the era of peace on earth in words similar to Isaiah's (cf. Isa 52:7). In his second chapter, Nahum graphically and dramatically described the invasion and overthrow of Nineveh; and in the third, he told why that destruction was deserved and decreed (BD, "Nahum").
quotes.speaker: Ellis T. Rasmussen
quotes.source: A Latter-day Saint Commentary on the Old Testament
quotes.attr: Ellis T. Rasmussen, A Latter-day Saint Commentary on the Old Testament, 665-666
